news_summary: Today's environment news highlights innovative solutions for sustainability, increased support for clean energy projects, the integration of Indigenous knowledge in scientific efforts, and youth-driven progress toward sustainability goals.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des solutions innovantes en matière de développement durable, un soutien accru aux projets d'énergie propre, l'intégration des savoirs autochtones dans les travaux scientifiques, ainsi que les avancées menées par les jeunes en faveur des objectifs de développement durable.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan soluciones innovadoras para la sostenibilidad, un mayor apoyo a los proyectos de energía limpia, la integración de los conocimientos indígenas en la investigación científica y los avances impulsados por los jóvenes hacia los objetivos de sostenibilidad.

articles.title: Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Liquidonate CEO Disney Petit On Solving The Retail Returns Crisis
articles.summary:
Subscribe to receive transcripts by email. Read along with this episode. What if the solution...
The post Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Liquidonate CEO Disney Petit On Solving The Retail Returns Crisis appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Subscribe to receive transcripts by email. Read along with this episode.

What if the solution to the retail industry’s $890 billion returns crisis wasn’t better logistics, but better logic? Disney Petit, founder and CEO of Liquidonate, is proving that the most sustainable return skips the trip back to a warehouse and goes directly to a community in need. Americans returned nearly 17% of all retail purchases last year, generating 2.6 million tons of landfill waste and 16 million tons of CO2 emissions. Each return costs retailers between $25 and $35 to process, yet 52% of consumers admit to participating in return fraud at least once. Petit witnessed this broken system firsthand as employee number 15 at Postmates, where she built the customer service team and created Civic Labs, the company’s social responsibility arm. Her food security product Bento, which allowed people without smartphones to access free food via text message, won Time Magazine’s 2021 Invention of the Year Award. Now Liquidonate has earned recognition as one of Time’s Best Inventions of 2025.

Liquidonate integrates directly with retailers’ existing warehouse and return management systems. When a product comes back and can’t be resold—open box, slightly damaged, or simply unwanted—the platform automatically matches it with a local nonprofit or school that needs it. “It’s the same reverse logistics workflow they already use,” Petit explains. “It’s just redirected toward community good instead of going to the landfill.” The platform handles everything: shipping labels, pickup coordination, and tax documentation so retailers can write off donations. Retailers recover logistics costs through tax benefits while communities receive quality products, and millions of pounds of goods stay out of landfills.

To date, retailers using Liquidonate have diverted over 12 million items from landfills, working with more than 4,000 nonprofits across the country. Liquidonate also tackles return fraud by eliminating “keep it” returns, when customers claim they want to return something but are told to keep the item and still receive a refund. “One hundred percent of the time we’re producing a shipping label for a nonprofit who wants that product,” Petit says. “We completely eliminate that keep-it return option, so we eliminate the returns fraud option.” With $900 billion worth of inventory potentially available for redirection, Petit approaches the business through the lens of environmental justice, building a for-profit company designed to prove that doing good and doing well aren’t mutually exclusive—they’re interdependent.

Nonprofits and schools can sign up for free at liquidonate.com. Retailers interested in partnering can reach out to partners@liquidonate.com.

Subscribe to Sustainability In Your Ear on iTunes

Follow Sustainability In Your Ear on Spreaker, iHeartRadio, or YouTube

Editor’s Note: This episode originally aired on November 17, 2025.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and innovative solution to the large-scale environmental problem of retail returns waste, which generates millions of tons of landfill waste and CO2 emissions. Liquidonate's platform redirects unsellable returned goods to nonprofits and schools, reducing landfill waste, cutting emissions, and supporting communities. The impact is broad, measurable, and addresses both environmental sustainability and social good, with clear operational details and substantial scale.
articles.personality_title: Liquidonate helps turn retail returns into donations, cutting waste and fraud
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, many items bought in stores or online are returned. In the US alone, shoppers returned nearly 17% of all purchases last year. This created a big problem: millions of tons of trash and pollution, plus high costs for stores to handle these returns. Some customers even trick stores by returning items but keeping them anyway.

**What happened** – Disney Petit, CEO of a company called Liquidonate, found a new way to fix this problem. Instead of sending returned items back to warehouses or throwing them away, Liquidonate connects stores directly to local charities and schools that can use these goods. The system works with stores’ current return processes and takes care of shipping and paperwork so donations are easy and beneficial for everyone.

**Impact** – This approach stops millions of products from becoming trash and helps communities get useful items. So far, Liquidonate has saved over 12 million items from landfills and worked with more than 4,000 nonprofits. It also stops a common trick where customers get refunds but keep the products, helping stores save money. The company has been recognized by Time magazine for its smart and practical invention.

**What's next step** – More stores can join Liquidonate to reduce waste and support local groups. Nonprofits and schools can sign up for free to receive donated items. This idea could grow bigger, changing how returns are handled across the country and reducing environmental harm.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Liquidonate turns unsellable retail returns into valuable donations, cutting waste, saving money, and helping communities.
articles.personality_title_fr: Liquidonate transforme les retours en dons pour réduire les déchets et la fraude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, de nombreux articles achetés en magasin ou en ligne sont retournés. Aux États-Unis, près de 17 % des achats ont été retournés l'année dernière. Cela crée un gros problème : des millions de tonnes de déchets et de pollution, ainsi que des coûts élevés pour les magasins. Certains clients trompent même les magasins en retournant des articles tout en les gardant.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Disney Petit, PDG de Liquidonate, a trouvé une nouvelle solution. Au lieu de renvoyer les articles retournés en entrepôt ou de les jeter, Liquidonate met en relation les magasins avec des associations locales et des écoles qui peuvent utiliser ces produits. Le système fonctionne avec les processus de retour existants des magasins et s’occupe de l’expédition et des documents pour faciliter les dons.

**Impact** – Cette méthode empêche des millions de produits de devenir des déchets et aide les communautés à obtenir des articles utiles. Jusqu’à présent, Liquidonate a sauvé plus de 12 millions d’articles des décharges et travaillé avec plus de 4 000 associations. Elle bloque aussi une fraude où les clients obtiennent un remboursement tout en gardant le produit, ce qui fait économiser de l’argent aux magasins. L’entreprise a été reconnue par le magazine Time pour son invention intelligente.

**Prochaine étape** – Plus de magasins peuvent rejoindre Liquidonate pour réduire les déchets et soutenir les groupes locaux. Les associations et écoles peuvent s’inscrire gratuitement pour recevoir des dons. Cette idée pourrait se développer et changer la gestion des retours dans tout le pays.

**Une phrase clé** – Liquidonate transforme les retours invendables en dons utiles, réduisant les déchets, économisant de l’argent et aidant les communautés.
articles.personality_title_es: Liquidonate convierte devoluciones en donaciones para reducir residuos y fraudes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, muchas cosas que se compran en tiendas o en línea se devuelven. Solo en Estados Unidos, casi el 17 % de las compras fueron devueltas el año pasado. Esto genera un gran problema: millones de toneladas de basura y contaminación, además de altos costos para las tiendas. Algunos clientes incluso engañan a las tiendas devolviendo productos pero quedándoselos.

**Qué pasó** – Disney Petit, directora de Liquidonate, encontró una solución nueva. En lugar de enviar los productos devueltos a almacenes o tirarlos, Liquidonate conecta las tiendas directamente con organizaciones benéficas y escuelas locales que pueden usar esos productos. El sistema funciona con los procesos de devoluciones que ya usan las tiendas y se encarga del envío y los papeles para facilitar las donaciones.

**Impacto** – Esta idea evita que millones de productos terminen en la basura y ayuda a las comunidades a obtener cosas útiles. Hasta ahora, Liquidonate ha salvado más de 12 millones de artículos de los vertederos y ha trabajado con más de 4,000 organizaciones. También detiene un fraude común donde los clientes devuelven productos pero se quedan con ellos, ayudando a las tiendas a ahorrar dinero. La empresa fue reconocida por la revista Time por su invento práctico y útil.

**Próximo paso** – Más tiendas pueden unirse a Liquidonate para reducir residuos y apoyar a grupos locales. Las organizaciones benéficas y escuelas pueden inscribirse gratis para recibir donaciones. Esta idea podría crecer y cambiar la forma en que se manejan las devoluciones en todo el país.

**Una frase para recordar** – Liquidonate convierte devoluciones no vendibles en donaciones valiosas, reduciendo residuos, ahorrando dinero y ayudando a las comunidades.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a flowing conveyor belt seamlessly transforming returned retail packages into vibrant community care parcels being joyfully received by stylized school and nonprofit building silhouettes, with subtle green leaves and recycling arrows gently woven into the scene to symbolize sustainability and the circular flow of goods.

articles.title: Many companies want clean energy. Georgia Power will soon let them build it.
articles.content:
This coverage is made possible through a partnership between Grist and WABE, Atlanta’s NPR station.

Many corporations have pledged to reduce their greenhouse gas emissions, but when it comes to energy generation, they have struggled to meet those goals because they have to get power from the grid. Utilities and regulators largely determine how that energy is generated, and most still use fossil fuel sources like coal and natural gas alongside renewable energy.

Now, Georgia’s largest utility, Georgia Power, is launching a new Customer-Identified Resource program that aims to change that by letting companies propose and fund their own clean energy projects. It passed with bipartisan support from the state’s public service commissioners on April 7, and is expected to open by this summer.

“It provides an opportunity for the first time for these customers to be able to identify and bring projects to Georgia Power,” said Priya Barua, a senior director of Utility Partnerships and Innovation at the Corporate Energy Buyers Association, or CEBA. She worked with the utility and other stakeholders on developing the new initiative.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Companies with emissions-reduction targets, like CEBA’s members, are often interested in building their own clean energy projects to help meet climate goals. Meta, for instance, has built solar fields in Georgia to help power its data center complex in Social Circle. But that complex gets its additional energy from an electric membership cooperative, not Georgia Power.

Prior to this new program, there hasn’t been a mechanism for Georgia Power customers to bring similar projects to that utility’s grid. In fact, Hyundai purchased renewable energy credits from solar fields in Texas to offset the energy use at its plant near Savannah. Under the new program, Georgia Power customers will be able to build clean energy projects within the state. They can opt to fund clean energy projects that don’t get chosen during the utility’s regular bid process, or develop their own.

“The program allows multiple customers to be able to bring forward a project together, which really opens this program up to a broader range of small and medium sized commercial and industrial customers,” Barua said.

Supporters hope the program can be a model for other utilities around the country. Georgia already ranks eighth in the country for solar energy capacity, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association, but proponents of renewable energy have long argued the state could be adding more solar more quickly. Those arguments have gotten louder in recent years as Georgia Power has requested and received approval for large amounts of new energy generation to serve growing demand the utility expects to see, mostly from data centers. The utility is planning to generate most of that new energy from natural gas. Environmental and consumer advocates have argued that some or all of the projected demand can be met with renewables and efficiency programs instead.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

This program, Barua said, could help cover the new demand the utility is predicting.

“It just accelerates the clean energy projects coming to the system, which would then negate the need for natural gas and other types of generation resources down the road,” she said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Georgia Power's new program allowing customers to propose and fund clean energy projects, which represents a significant and tangible step toward increasing renewable energy capacity and reducing reliance on fossil fuels. This initiative has broad implications for climate action and could serve as a model for other utilities, thus having meaningful positive impact on the environment and society.
articles.personality_title: Georgia Power opens doors for customers to build clean energy projects
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many companies want to reduce pollution by using clean energy, but they usually have to buy electricity from big utility companies. These utilities often rely on coal and natural gas, which produce greenhouse gases.

**What happened** – Georgia Power, the largest utility in Georgia, created a new program called Customer-Identified Resource. Starting this summer, companies can suggest and pay for their own clean energy projects to connect to Georgia Power’s grid. This program was approved on April 7 with support from different political groups.

**Impact** – This program allows businesses to build solar or other clean energy sources right in Georgia, instead of buying renewable energy credits from far away. It also lets small and medium-sized companies join together to create projects. This could help Georgia add more solar power faster and reduce the need for new natural gas plants.

**What's next step** – The program will start accepting proposals this summer. If it works well, other utilities in the U.S. might try similar programs, helping more clean energy projects get built.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Georgia Power’s new program lets customers directly create clean energy projects, making it easier to grow renewable energy in the state.
articles.personality_title_fr: Georgia Power ouvre la voie aux clients pour construire des projets d'énergie propre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses entreprises veulent réduire leur pollution en utilisant de l'énergie propre, mais elles doivent souvent acheter de l'électricité auprès de grandes compagnies d'électricité. Ces compagnies utilisent souvent du charbon et du gaz naturel, qui produisent des gaz à effet de serre.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Georgia Power, la plus grande compagnie d'électricité en Géorgie, a créé un nouveau programme appelé Ressource Identifiée par le Client. À partir de cet été, les entreprises pourront proposer et financer leurs propres projets d'énergie propre pour les connecter au réseau de Georgia Power. Ce programme a été approuvé le 7 avril avec le soutien de différents groupes politiques.

**Impact** – Ce programme permet aux entreprises de construire des sources d'énergie solaire ou autres sources propres directement en Géorgie, au lieu d'acheter des crédits d'énergie renouvelable venant de loin. Il permet aussi aux petites et moyennes entreprises de s'associer pour créer des projets. Cela pourrait aider la Géorgie à ajouter plus d'énergie solaire plus rapidement et réduire le besoin de nouvelles centrales à gaz naturel.

**Prochaine étape** – Le programme commencera à accepter des propositions cet été. S'il fonctionne bien, d'autres compagnies d'électricité aux États-Unis pourraient essayer des programmes similaires, aidant ainsi à construire plus de projets d'énergie propre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau programme de Georgia Power permet aux clients de créer directement des projets d'énergie propre, facilitant ainsi le développement des énergies renouvelables dans l'État.
articles.personality_title_es: Georgia Power permite a los clientes construir proyectos de energía limpia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas empresas quieren reducir la contaminación usando energía limpia, pero generalmente tienen que comprar electricidad a grandes compañías eléctricas. Estas compañías suelen usar carbón y gas natural, que producen gases de efecto invernadero.

**Qué pasó** – Georgia Power, la compañía eléctrica más grande de Georgia, creó un nuevo programa llamado Recurso Identificado por el Cliente. A partir de este verano, las empresas podrán proponer y financiar sus propios proyectos de energía limpia para conectarlos a la red de Georgia Power. El programa fue aprobado el 7 de abril con apoyo de diferentes grupos políticos.

**Impacto** – Este programa permite que las empresas construyan fuentes de energía solar u otras fuentes limpias directamente en Georgia, en lugar de comprar créditos de energía renovable de lugares lejanos. También permite que pequeñas y medianas empresas se unan para crear proyectos. Esto podría ayudar a Georgia a agregar energía solar más rápido y reducir la necesidad de nuevas plantas de gas natural.

**Próximo paso** – El programa comenzará a aceptar propuestas este verano. Si funciona bien, otras compañías eléctricas en Estados Unidos podrían probar programas similares, ayudando a construir más proyectos de energía limpia.

**Resumen en una frase** – El nuevo programa de Georgia Power permite a los clientes crear proyectos de energía limpia directamente, facilitando el crecimiento de las energías renovables en el estado.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant Georgia landscape featuring stylized solar panels and wind turbines arranged like puzzle pieces fitting together, glowing softly under a golden sky, with abstract silhouettes of interconnected buildings symbolizing companies collaborating to build clean energy projects, all rendered in natural, earthy tones.

articles.title: Why Western scientists are turning to Indigenous knowledge
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Marco Hatch describes his own work with characteristic dry humor: “I’m a glorified clam counter.” What he’s actually doing is more complicated. As a marine ecologist at Western Washington University and an enrolled member of the Samish Indian Nation, Hatch is collaborating with seven Indigenous communities to rebuild clam […]
The post Why Western scientists are turning to Indigenous knowledge first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Marco Hatch describes his own work with characteristic dry humor: “I’m a glorified clam counter.” What he’s actually doing is more complicated. As a marine ecologist at Western Washington University and an enrolled member of the Samish Indian Nation, Hatch is collaborating with seven Indigenous communities to rebuild clam gardens in the Pacific Northwest: terraced, rock-walled beaches that their ancestors engineered at least 4,000 years ago. Butter clams, red rock crab, basket cockles, sea cucumbers, seaweed. His data helps these nations secure the permits they need to maintain or re-engineer the structures. It also helps them reclaim food sovereignty and access to places that colonization, land privatization, and forced boarding school confinements put out of reach.

Kyle Whyte, a professor of environmental justice at the University of Michigan and member of the Citizen Potawatomi Nation, calls this a “massive shift” in how Western scientists relate to Indigenous knowledge. Historically, Western researchers classified traditional Native thought as mythic, religious, or fabricated. The reckoning with that position has taken a long time.

Millions spent confirming what communities already knew

The cost of that dismissal is measurable. “Often in science, you see millions of dollars being invested in [one study] that, at the end of the day, might just produce a very simple result that Indigenous people have known for generations,” Whyte said. “It suggests that if there was more collaboration, we could not only save money, but we could stand on the shoulders of Indigenous people and start doing more advanced studies about the ecosystem.”

In British Columbia, researchers at Simon Fraser University found that when Indigenous groups tended forest gardens, they produced an impressive range of food plants, including crabapple, hazelnut, wild plum, wild rice, and cranberries, and improved overall forest health in the process. In Michigan, Whyte has worked with the Sault Ste Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians to restore prescribed burns, which many Native nations historically used to support populations of sharp-tailed grouse, snowshoe hare, and deer. Those populations declined after the federal government banned burning in 1911. After more than 20 ecology surveys, the tribe made its case.

Kisha Supernant, who is Métis and Papaschase and directs the University of Alberta’s Institute of Prairie and Indigenous Archaeology, describes Indigenous knowledge as containing “a rich history of observation, experimentation and understanding that has its own systems of rigor.” That rigor comes from a fundamentally different way of organizing knowledge. Western science works in silos: ecology, biology, geology. Indigenous knowledge treats “the earth and the water and the air and the plants and the animals as deeply interdependent and interconnected; to understand one is to understand all. And that has a lot to teach Western science.”

The frustration of having to prove what you already know

The collaboration is growing, but it still carries a structural problem: Indigenous communities often have to validate their practices using Western scientific methods before they can act on them.

Suzanne Greenlaw, a citizen of the Houlton Band of Maliseet Indians and an ecologist at the Schoodic Institute, participated in a 2016 study on how sweet grass rebounds after harvesting, part of a Wabanaki effort to reclaim the right to gather it from Acadia National Park land in Maine. They had been cut off from those ancestral marshes for at least 100 years. Non-Indigenous researchers initially chose study plots with no connection to those once used by the community. The resulting comparison showed Wabanaki practitioners understood better than anyone how and where to harvest for the greatest ecological benefit. Harvest rights may be reclaimed later this year.

Greenlaw is now working on a restoration project in Acadia’s freshwater marshlands, using pollen from core samples to identify plants like cattails and groundnuts that Wabanaki communities once ate. The National Park Service data was scattered across spreadsheets “that didn’t tell a continuous story” of the landscape over time. “The question from Wabanaki people has been, ‘What is the land telling us? What is the story of the landscape? What is the story of plants that were here?” Greenlaw said. Whether the community will need separate permits for each food source or can be granted broader harvest rights over an entire freshwater habitat is still unresolved. “Wabanaki people have been here for seven generations, to caretake the landscape,” Greenlaw said. “The idea that we would harm [it] has not shown up in any examples we can give.”

Who sets the terms

For Supernant, the shape of the collaboration matters as much as the fact of it. “If the burden of proof remains on Indigenous communities to demonstrate, using Western scientific methods, that their knowledge is valid, I think we’re not at the place we need to be. It is difficult to braid two things together when they’re not given equal weight in the braid.” Greenlaw was more direct: “Western science can help, as long as Native people are still decision makers.”

Canada’s 2019 legislation requiring the consideration of Indigenous knowledge in fisheries regulatory decisions is one sign of progress. Federal funding since 2022 has supported research into how the two knowledge systems can work together while each remains distinct.

Whyte’s prescription for Western scientists is specific: approach potential Indigenous partners before research questions exist. “Let’s just get excited together about the topic, and plan from the beginning,” he said. “Indigenous people need to be involved at the earliest stages of research.”

On the beaches of the Pacific Northwest, Hatch has seen what that openness produces. When Indigenous knowledge holders and land managers meet around a shared goal of reconnecting to place, “a lot of relationships are strengthened, and those connections are sprouting into new areas,” he said. Memories surface in elders and are passed to younger generations. “The beach is a great place to connect.”

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful collaboration between Western scientists and Indigenous communities to restore ecosystems and reclaim traditional ecological knowledge, which has broad environmental and cultural benefits. It details specific projects with measurable impacts, such as rebuilding clam gardens and restoring prescribed burns, and discusses policy progress recognizing Indigenous knowledge. This represents a significant positive shift in environmental science and Indigenous rights.
articles.personality_title: Scientists and Indigenous communities team up to restore nature and culture
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For a long time, Western scientists ignored Indigenous knowledge, seeing it as myth or religion instead of valuable information. This caused delays and extra costs in environmental research and conservation.

**What happened** – Marco Hatch, a marine ecologist and member of the Samish Indian Nation, is working with seven Indigenous communities in the Pacific Northwest to rebuild ancient clam gardens. These gardens are rock-walled beach terraces that help sea life thrive. His research helps these communities get permits to care for these areas and reclaim access to traditional food sources. Similar projects include forest gardening in British Columbia and restoring controlled burns in Michigan, both led by Indigenous groups with Western scientists’ support.

**Impact** – This teamwork shows a new respect for Indigenous knowledge, which treats nature as an interconnected whole, unlike Western science’s separate categories. It saves money and time by building on what Indigenous people already know. It also helps communities regain control over their land and traditions, which were taken away by colonization and laws.

**What's next step** – More laws, like Canada’s 2019 rule requiring Indigenous knowledge in fisheries decisions, encourage this kind of partnership. Experts suggest involving Indigenous people from the very start of research projects. This will create stronger relationships, better science, and more chances to protect nature and culture together.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Working together, Western scientists and Indigenous communities are restoring ecosystems and honoring traditional knowledge to benefit both nature and people.
articles.personality_title_fr: Scientifiques et communautés autochtones unissent leurs forces pour restaurer la nature et la culture
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, les scientifiques occidentaux ont ignoré les savoirs autochtones, les considérant comme des mythes ou des croyances religieuses plutôt que comme des informations précieuses. Cela a entraîné des retards et des coûts supplémentaires dans la recherche et la conservation de l’environnement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Marco Hatch, écologiste marin et membre de la nation Samish, collabore avec sept communautés autochtones du Nord-Ouest pacifique pour reconstruire d’anciens jardins de palourdes. Ces jardins sont des terrasses de plage en pierre qui favorisent la vie marine. Ses recherches aident ces communautés à obtenir des permis pour entretenir ces espaces et retrouver l’accès à leurs sources alimentaires traditionnelles. D’autres projets similaires incluent des jardins forestiers en Colombie-Britannique et la restauration des brûlages contrôlés dans le Michigan, dirigés par des groupes autochtones avec le soutien de scientifiques occidentaux.

**Impact** – Cette collaboration montre un nouveau respect pour les savoirs autochtones, qui considèrent la nature comme un tout interconnecté, contrairement à la science occidentale qui divise en catégories séparées. Cela permet d’économiser temps et argent en s’appuyant sur ce que les peuples autochtones savent déjà. Cela aide aussi ces communautés à reprendre le contrôle de leurs terres et traditions, enlevés par la colonisation et des lois.

**Prochaine étape** – Des lois comme celle du Canada en 2019, qui exige la prise en compte des savoirs autochtones dans les décisions sur la pêche, encouragent ce type de partenariat. Les experts recommandent d’associer les peuples autochtones dès le début des projets de recherche. Cela renforcera les relations, améliorera la science et protégera ensemble la nature et la culture.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En travaillant ensemble, scientifiques occidentaux et communautés autochtones restaurent les écosystèmes et valorisent les savoirs traditionnels pour le bien de la nature et des peuples.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos y comunidades indígenas se unen para restaurar la naturaleza y la cultura
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos occidentales ignoraron el conocimiento indígena, viéndolo como mito o religión en lugar de información valiosa. Esto causó retrasos y costos adicionales en la investigación y conservación ambiental.

**Qué pasó** – Marco Hatch, ecólogo marino y miembro de la Nación Samish, trabaja con siete comunidades indígenas en el noroeste del Pacífico para reconstruir antiguos jardines de almejas. Estos jardines son terrazas en la playa hechas con muros de piedra que ayudan a que la vida marina prospere. Su investigación ayuda a estas comunidades a obtener permisos para cuidar estas áreas y recuperar acceso a sus fuentes tradicionales de alimento. Proyectos similares incluyen jardines forestales en Columbia Británica y restauración de quemas controladas en Michigan, liderados por grupos indígenas con apoyo de científicos occidentales.

**Impacto** – Esta colaboración muestra un nuevo respeto por el conocimiento indígena, que ve la naturaleza como un todo interconectado, a diferencia de la ciencia occidental que la divide en partes separadas. Ahorra dinero y tiempo al construir sobre lo que ya saben los pueblos indígenas. También ayuda a las comunidades a recuperar el control de sus tierras y tradiciones, que fueron arrebatadas por la colonización y leyes.

**Próximo paso** – Más leyes, como la de Canadá en 2019 que exige considerar el conocimiento indígena en decisiones pesqueras, fomentan este tipo de asociación. Los expertos sugieren involucrar a los pueblos indígenas desde el inicio de los proyectos de investigación. Esto creará relaciones más fuertes, mejor ciencia y más oportunidades para proteger la naturaleza y la cultura juntos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Trabajando juntos, científicos occidentales y comunidades indígenas están restaurando ecosistemas y honrando conocimientos tradicionales para beneficiar a la naturaleza y a las personas.
articles.image_prompt: A serene coastal scene at sunset featuring terraced clam gardens with rock walls gently curving along the shoreline, interwoven with symbolic elements like basket cockles, butter clams, red rock crabs, and seaweed subtly integrated into the landscape, while softly glowing silhouettes of interconnected water, earth, and air currents flow above, representing the harmonious blending of Indigenous knowledge and Western science.

articles.title: Tobago’s teen archery champion takes aim at sustainability targets
articles.summary: Six-time Trinidad and Tobago archery champion, Anthurium Lewis, has told UN News how sport helped her overcome age barriers in environmental advocacy and how in the future it can contribute to reaching globally agreed poverty and sustainability goals.
articles.content:
Aged 17 and coaching archers three times her age on her home Caribbean island of Tobago, Ms. Lewis transfers the confidence and skills she gained from the sport into advocacy and diplomacy, spaces where youth, especially girls, must often fight to have their voices heard.

“The biggest challenge has to be my age,” she said, highlighting a cultural norm passed down to many young Caribbeans to “speak only when you're spoken to” and to “leave the older heads to have the big discussions.”

Sport has helped her overcome these barriers: “Archery gives you that discipline to keep pushing forward,” she said.

United Nations Anthurium Lewis receives top prize at an archery competition.

Now, Ms. Lewis wants to use her platform as a Young Leader for the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) – the internationally agreed targets aimed at boosting peace and prosperity for people and the planet – to show that young people can lead change when the world makes space for their voices and equips them with the right skills.

Sport, she believes, is one of the most powerful ways to develop these skills.

‘Children can do it’

We have a say. We have the intellect. We have the passion behind it as well. You don't have to wait until you're 25 to want to fight for a cause...We can start right now.

The most profound transformation sport brings is quieter than a trophy or a title; it begins in the mind.

She believes that archery is 80 per cent a test of mentality with the self-esteem and discipline it builds in a young person transferring into all parts of their life.

However, acknowledging the challenges other young people face with powerful ideas but nowhere to turn, Ms. Lewis is encouraging her peers to have confidence in their voice.

“Children can do it. We have a say. We have the intellect. We have the passion behind it as well. You don't have to wait until you're 25 to want to fight for a cause...We can start right now.”

Sustainability mission

Through her foundation, Lewis is on a mission to plant native fruit trees across Tobago to bring the island toward more sustainable practices and boost food security.

She also visits schools, communities and faith-based organizations, where she works to empower young people to plant fruit trees, such as sapodilla, soursop, custard apple, breadfruit and starfruit.

From Fruit Trees to Food Security: Anthurium Lewis | United Nations

“You won't just find me planting,” she said, “You'll actually find other young people, very young children as well, going out there and planting their own fruit trees that they will look after.”

Efforts pay off

On her first visit to one rural primary school in Tobago, Lewis arrived to find the school's gardening area overgrown and abandoned, but two months later, she returned to find it transformed: it was clear and full of children diligently tending to their plants during the school day.

“I was quite pleased by that change,” she said, emphasising that the work also gives children a chance to step outside the noise of the classroom, connect with their peers and build something together with their own hands.

For Ms. Lewis, that sense of ownership, that fosters discipline and self-confidence, is the whole point.

Anthurium Lewis spoke to UN News ahead of the annual ECOSOC Youth Forum (April 14-16, 2026) which brings young leaders together with policymakers, to focus on advancing the 2030 Agenda and Sustainable Development Goals.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a young archery champion using her sport-derived skills to overcome social barriers and actively promote environmental sustainability and food security on her island through tree planting. Her efforts have tangible community impact, empowering youth and contributing to global sustainability goals, with clear context and significance beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: Tobago’s teen archery champion leads youth in planting for a greener future
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Anthurium Lewis is a 17-year-old archery champion from Tobago, part of Trinidad and Tobago in the Caribbean. Despite her young age, she coaches older archers and uses the confidence from her sport to speak up about important issues like the environment and poverty.

**What happened** – Ms. Lewis shared how archery helped her break cultural rules that often silence young people, especially girls. She now works as a Young Leader for the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Through her own foundation, she encourages young people to plant native fruit trees on Tobago to improve food security and promote sustainable living.

**Impact** – Her work has changed communities. For example, a school garden that was once neglected became a lively place where children care for plants, learning responsibility and teamwork. By combining sport, leadership, and environmental action, Ms. Lewis shows how young people can make a real difference locally and help meet global goals.

**What's next step** – Ms. Lewis will continue planting fruit trees and inspiring youth across Tobago. She will also take part in the United Nations’ ECOSOC Youth Forum in April 2026, where young leaders meet policymakers to discuss ways to advance the 2030 Agenda for sustainable development.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A young archery champion from Tobago is using her sport skills to empower youth and promote tree planting for a healthier, more sustainable island community.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une championne d'archerie adolescente de Tobago guide la jeunesse vers un avenir plus vert
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Anthurium Lewis est une championne d'archerie de 17 ans originaire de Tobago, dans les Caraïbes. Malgré son jeune âge, elle entraîne des archers plus âgés et utilise la confiance acquise grâce à son sport pour s'exprimer sur des sujets importants comme l'environnement et la pauvreté.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Mme Lewis a expliqué comment l'archerie l'a aidée à dépasser des règles culturelles qui empêchent souvent les jeunes, surtout les filles, de s'exprimer. Elle travaille maintenant comme Jeune Leader pour les Objectifs de Développement Durable (ODD) des Nations Unies. Par sa fondation, elle encourage les jeunes à planter des arbres fruitiers indigènes à Tobago pour améliorer la sécurité alimentaire et promouvoir un mode de vie durable.

**Impact** – Son travail a transformé des communautés. Par exemple, un jardin d'école autrefois abandonné est devenu un lieu vivant où les enfants s'occupent des plantes, apprenant la responsabilité et le travail d'équipe. En combinant sport, leadership et action environnementale, Mme Lewis montre comment les jeunes peuvent faire une vraie différence locale et contribuer aux objectifs mondiaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Mme Lewis continuera à planter des arbres fruitiers et à inspirer les jeunes de Tobago. Elle participera également au Forum des Jeunes du Conseil économique et social des Nations Unies (ECOSOC) en avril 2026, où les jeunes leaders rencontrent des décideurs pour avancer dans l'Agenda 2030 du développement durable.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une jeune championne d'archerie de Tobago utilise ses compétences sportives pour responsabiliser la jeunesse et promouvoir la plantation d'arbres pour une communauté insulaire plus saine et durable.
articles.personality_title_es: La campeona adolescente de tiro con arco de Tobago lidera a los jóvenes en la plantación para un futuro más verde
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Anthurium Lewis es una campeona de tiro con arco de 17 años de Tobago, parte de Trinidad y Tobago en el Caribe. A pesar de su juventud, entrena a arqueros mayores y usa la confianza que le da el deporte para hablar sobre temas importantes como el medio ambiente y la pobreza.

**Qué pasó** – La señorita Lewis contó cómo el tiro con arco le ayudó a superar reglas culturales que a menudo silencian a los jóvenes, especialmente a las niñas. Ahora trabaja como Líder Joven para los Objetivos de Desarrollo Sostenible (ODS) de las Naciones Unidas. A través de su propia fundación, anima a los jóvenes a plantar árboles frutales nativos en Tobago para mejorar la seguridad alimentaria y promover un estilo de vida sostenible.

**Impacto** – Su trabajo ha cambiado comunidades. Por ejemplo, un jardín escolar que estaba descuidado se convirtió en un lugar activo donde los niños cuidan las plantas, aprendiendo responsabilidad y trabajo en equipo. Combinando deporte, liderazgo y acción ambiental, la señorita Lewis muestra cómo los jóvenes pueden hacer una diferencia real localmente y ayudar a cumplir metas globales.

**Próximo paso** – La señorita Lewis continuará plantando árboles frutales e inspirando a los jóvenes de Tobago. También participará en el Foro Juvenil del ECOSOC de las Naciones Unidas en abril de 2026, donde líderes jóvenes se reúnen con responsables políticos para avanzar en la Agenda 2030 para el desarrollo sostenible.

**Frase clave** – Una joven campeona de tiro con arco de Tobago usa sus habilidades deportivas para empoderar a los jóvenes y promover la plantación de árboles para una comunidad isleña más saludable y sostenible.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a young archer’s silhouette poised with a bow and arrow made of vibrant fruit tree branches, aiming toward a lush Caribbean island landscape where native fruit trees like sapodilla, soursop, and breadfruit grow abundantly, surrounded by the silhouettes of children planting and nurturing seedlings under a soft, natural color palette.
